question: You find an unconscious patient lying on their back. When you open the airway with head-tilt chin-lift you hear no breath sounds and see no chest movement. You give two rescue breaths but the chest does not rise. What should you do next?
option_a: Give more forceful breaths
option_b: Reposition the head and try again. If airway is not adequately opened breaths will not enter. Ensure adequate head extension and chin lift then reattempt ventilation
rationale: If initial breaths do not produce chest rise the most common cause is improper head positioning. Reposition ensuring adequate head extension and chin lift. If breaths still do not enter consider airway obstruction and follow choking protocols.

question: An unresponsive patient has sonorous respirations that sound like snoring. What is the most likely cause and what is your intervention?
option_a: The patient is just sleeping deeply
option_b: The tongue is falling back and partially blocking the airway. Perform head-tilt chin-lift or jaw thrust to lift the tongue forward and open the airway
option_d: Place a pillow under their head
rationale: Sonorous respirations or snoring in an unconscious patient indicates the tongue has fallen back against the posterior pharynx. Head-tilt chin-lift lifts the tongue by moving the mandible forward. This is the most common cause of airway obstruction in unconscious patients.

question: A patient with facial burns from a house fire has singed nasal hairs hoarse voice and is coughing black sputum. His breathing is currently adequate with SpO2 of 96%. Why are these findings concerning?
option_a: They are minor findings since his oxygen is good
option_b: These signs indicate inhalation injury. The airway can swell over hours and completely obstruct. Current good oxygenation does not predict future airway status. Alert hospital for possible emergency airway intervention
option_c: The black sputum will clear with coughing
option_d: Hoarseness will resolve on its own
rationale: Singed nasal hairs soot in sputum and hoarse voice indicate thermal and smoke inhalation injury. Progressive airway edema can cause complete obstruction hours later. Current SpO2 does not predict airway deterioration. Early hospital notification is essential.

question: You need to insert an OPA in an unconscious adult patient. How do you select the correct size?
option_a: Measure from the corner of the mouth to the earlobe or angle of the jaw. This ensures the OPA is long enough to hold the tongue forward but not so long it pushes the epiglottis closed
option_b: Always use the largest size available
option_c: Measure from the lips to the chin
option_d: Use the smallest size to avoid injury
rationale: Correct OPA sizing is critical. Measure from the corner of the mouth to the earlobe or angle of the jaw. Too small will not hold the tongue. Too large can push the epiglottis down and obstruct the airway or cause trauma.

question: You begin inserting an OPA in an unresponsive patient. As the device touches the back of his throat he gags and starts to vomit. What should you do?
option_a: Push the OPA in quickly before more vomiting
option_b: Remove the OPA immediately. The gag reflex indicates the patient is not deeply enough unconscious for OPA. Suction vomit turn patient to side and consider NPA instead
option_c: Hold the OPA in place until vomiting stops
option_d: This reaction is normal continue insertion
rationale: OPA is contraindicated in patients with a gag reflex. Insertion can stimulate vomiting and aspiration. If gagging occurs remove OPA immediately suction the airway position to drain secretions. Consider NPA which is better tolerated in patients with intact gag reflex.

question: What is the correct technique for inserting an OPA in an adult patient?
option_a: Insert with tip pointing up rotating 180 degrees as it passes the tongue then advance until flange rests on lips
option_b: Insert straight in without rotation
rationale: For adults insert OPA with tip pointing toward roof of mouth. As it passes the tongue rotate 180 degrees so the curve follows the tongue contour. Advance until the flange rests on the lips. This prevents pushing the tongue backward.

question: An unconscious patient has a gag reflex and you cannot use an OPA. You decide to insert an NPA. How do you determine correct size?
option_a: Measure from tip of nose to earlobe or corner of mouth. Diameter should approximate the patient's little finger or nostril size
option_b: Use the same size as an OPA
rationale: NPA size is determined by measuring from the tip of the nose to the earlobe or corner of the mouth. Diameter should approximate the patient's little finger or nostril. Too long can enter the esophagus too short provides no benefit.

question: You are preparing to insert an NPA. Which nostril should you use and what direction should you insert it?
option_a: Either nostril. Insert perpendicular to face along floor of nasal cavity with bevel toward septum
option_b: Always use the left nostril
option_d: Insert with bevel away from septum
rationale: Insert NPA in the nostril that appears larger and unobstructed. Direct perpendicular to the face along the floor of nasal cavity not upward. Bevel should face the septum to reduce trauma to turbinates. Lubricate with water-soluble lubricant.

question: A trauma patient has clear fluid draining from his nose and blood behind his eardrums. You need to maintain his airway. Should you insert an NPA?
option_a: Yes NPAs are safe in all trauma patients
option_b: No. Clear fluid from nose with blood behind eardrums suggests basilar skull fracture. NPA is contraindicated as it could enter the cranial cavity. Use jaw thrust and consider OPA if no gag reflex
rationale: Suspected basilar skull fracture with CSF rhinorrhea is a contraindication to NPA. The cribriform plate may be fractured and NPA could enter the cranial vault. Use jaw thrust for airway maintenance. OPA is acceptable if no gag reflex.

question: An unconscious patient has copious vomit in their mouth and is making gurgling sounds with each breath. You have a portable suction unit. How should you suction the airway?
option_a: Suction continuously for as long as it takes to clear the airway
option_b: Suction for no more than 10-15 seconds at a time. Prolonged suctioning removes oxygen. If airway is not clear allow patient to breathe between attempts
option_c: Insert suction catheter as deep as possible
option_d: Suctioning is not needed if patient is breathing
rationale: Suction should be limited to 10-15 seconds to prevent hypoxia. If the airway is not clear pause to allow oxygenation then suction again. Use rigid catheter for thick secretions. Never insert beyond where you can see in the oropharynx.

question: You are suctioning an adult patient's airway with a rigid Yankauer catheter. How far should you insert it?
option_a: As deep as possible to get all secretions
option_b: Only as far as you can see. Do not blindly insert the catheter beyond the oropharynx as this can cause trauma vomiting or laryngospasm
option_c: To the level of the carina
rationale: Insert suction catheters only as far as you can see in adults. Blind deep insertion can cause trauma stimulate vomiting or trigger laryngospasm. Use sweeping motions to clear visible secretions in the oropharynx.

question: You are ventilating an apneic patient with a BVM connected to oxygen. What ventilation rate and volume should you deliver for an adult?
option_a: As fast as possible with maximum volume
option_b: 10-12 breaths per minute with just enough volume to see the chest rise lasting about 1 second per breath. Excessive rate or volume causes gastric insufflation
rationale: Adult ventilation rate is 10-12 breaths per minute one every 5-6 seconds. Each breath should last about 1 second with just enough volume to see chest rise. Excessive rate or volume causes gastric distension and aspiration risk.

question: You are alone and must ventilate an unresponsive patient with a BVM. What technique provides the best mask seal?
option_a: Use one hand in C-E grip on mask and squeeze bag with other hand. C shape holds mask while E shape lifts jaw
option_b: Hold mask with one finger
option_c: Place mask on face without holding it
option_d: Have family member hold the mask
rationale: Single-rescuer BVM requires the C-E technique. Thumb and index finger form a C around the mask connector creating seal. Remaining fingers form an E under the jaw lifting it into the mask. This is challenging and two rescuers are preferred.

question: Two EMRs are ventilating a patient. What is the advantage of two-person BVM ventilation over one-person technique?
option_a: No advantage both are equal
option_b: Two-person technique provides better mask seal and more effective ventilation. One person uses both hands to hold mask and maintain airway while the other squeezes the bag
option_c: Only one person should ever ventilate
rationale: Two-person BVM is more effective because one rescuer can use both hands to maintain mask seal and airway positioning while the second rescuer squeezes the bag. This results in better tidal volumes and less air leak.

question: You are providing BVM ventilation and notice the patient's stomach is becoming distended. What is the cause and how do you correct it?
option_a: This is normal during ventilation
option_b: Gastric insufflation from air entering stomach. Correct by repositioning airway ensuring adequate head tilt ventilating more slowly using less volume and giving breaths just until chest rise is visible
option_d: You need to suction the stomach
rationale: Gastric insufflation results from air entering the esophagus instead of trachea. Causes include excessive volume rapid delivery or poor airway positioning. Correct by repositioning head giving slower breaths and using only enough volume for visible chest rise.

question: A patient with mild respiratory distress has SpO2 of 92% on room air. She is alert and talking. What oxygen delivery device is most appropriate?
option_a: Non-rebreather mask at 15 LPM
option_b: Nasal cannula at 2-4 LPM. For mild hypoxia in an alert patient nasal cannula provides adequate supplementation while allowing the patient to talk and is more comfortable
option_d: No oxygen needed since she is talking
rationale: For mild hypoxia in alert patients nasal cannula at 2-4 LPM is appropriate. It delivers 24-44% oxygen allows conversation and is well-tolerated. Non-rebreather would be excessive for mild hypoxia. Target SpO2 of 94% or above.

question: A patient in severe respiratory distress has SpO2 of 82%. She is using accessory muscles and can only speak one word at a time. What oxygen delivery method is most appropriate?
option_a: Nasal cannula at 6 LPM
option_b: Non-rebreather mask at 15 LPM to deliver highest concentration of oxygen. Severe hypoxia requires high-flow oxygen. Monitor closely for deterioration requiring assisted ventilation
rationale: Severe hypoxia with signs of respiratory distress requires high-flow high-concentration oxygen. Non-rebreather mask at 15 LPM delivers approximately 90% oxygen. Monitor for further deterioration which would require assisted ventilation.

question: A 62-year-old male with COPD is in respiratory distress. SpO2 is 84%. He is on home oxygen at 2 LPM. Some providers worry about giving COPD patients high-flow oxygen. What should you do?
option_a: Withhold oxygen because COPD patients should never get high-flow oxygen
option_b: Give oxygen to maintain SpO2 around 88-92%. While some COPD patients rely on hypoxic drive never withhold oxygen from a hypoxic patient. Hypoxia kills faster than hypercapnia. Monitor for decreased respirations
option_c: Only give 2 LPM since that is his home setting
rationale: Never withhold oxygen from a hypoxic patient. While some COPD patients have hypoxic respiratory drive the risk of hypoxia outweighs CO2 retention risk. Target SpO2 of 88-92% in COPD patients and monitor for respiratory depression.

question: You place a nasal cannula on a patient at 6 LPM. She complains that her nose is becoming dry and uncomfortable. What can you do?
option_a: Remove the oxygen
option_b: Consider humidified oxygen if available for extended use or flows above 4 LPM. Nasal cannula flow above 4-6 LPM can dry mucous membranes. For short transport standard non-humidified oxygen is acceptable
rationale: High-flow nasal cannula can dry and irritate nasal mucosa. Humidified oxygen reduces discomfort. For short prehospital transports standard oxygen is acceptable. For extended use humidification improves comfort and tolerance.

question: A child with respiratory distress becomes very upset when you try to place an oxygen mask on her face. Fighting the mask makes her SpO2 drop further. What is an alternative approach?
option_a: Force the mask on because she needs oxygen
option_b: Use blow-by oxygen by holding the mask or tubing near her face without touching. A calm child receiving blow-by oxygen often does better than a crying child fighting a mask
rationale: Pediatric patients often do not tolerate masks. Blow-by oxygen held near the face provides some benefit without causing distress. A crying struggling child increases oxygen consumption potentially worsening hypoxia. Blow-by with calm child often achieves better oxygenation.

question: You are at a restaurant when a 50-year-old man stands up clutching his throat. He cannot speak cough or breathe and appears panicked. His face is turning blue. What should you do?
option_a: Perform back blows only
option_b: Perform abdominal thrusts Heimlich maneuver. Stand behind him wrap arms around waist make a fist above navel and deliver quick upward thrusts until object is expelled or he becomes unconscious
option_c: Encourage him to try to cough
rationale: Complete airway obstruction in a conscious adult is treated with abdominal thrusts. Inability to speak cough or breathe indicates complete obstruction. Continue thrusts until successful or patient becomes unconscious then begin CPR.

question: The choking victim becomes unconscious during your attempts to relieve the obstruction. He collapses to the floor. What should you do now?
option_a: Continue abdominal thrusts on the ground
option_b: Lower him carefully and begin CPR starting with chest compressions. Look in the mouth before each breath attempt and remove any visible object. Chest compressions may help dislodge the obstruction
option_d: Wait for EMS since he is unconscious
rationale: When a choking victim becomes unconscious begin CPR starting with chest compressions. Before each ventilation attempt look in the mouth and remove any visible object. Do not perform blind finger sweeps. Chest compressions can help dislodge foreign bodies.

question: A conscious adult has a partial airway obstruction. He is coughing forcefully and able to get some air. He appears distressed. What should you do?
option_a: Perform abdominal thrusts immediately
option_b: Encourage continued coughing. Do not interfere with effective coughing efforts. Stay with the patient and be ready to act if cough becomes ineffective or patient becomes unconscious
option_c: Give him water to wash down the obstruction
rationale: Partial obstruction with effective coughing should not be treated with abdominal thrusts which could convert partial to complete obstruction. Encourage coughing. Monitor closely and be prepared to intervene if cough becomes ineffective or patient deteriorates.

question: A mother hands you her 8-month-old baby who is choking and not breathing. The infant is conscious but cannot cry or cough. What is the correct technique to relieve choking in an infant?
option_a: Abdominal thrusts like an adult
option_b: Alternate 5 back blows and 5 chest thrusts. Support infant face down on forearm with head lower than body for back blows then turn face up for chest thrusts. Continue until object expelled or infant unconscious
rationale: Infant choking is treated with alternating 5 back blows and 5 chest thrusts not abdominal thrusts which can damage internal organs. Support infant properly with head lower than body. Continue cycles until successful or infant becomes unconscious.

question: The choking infant becomes unconscious in your arms. What should you do?
option_a: Continue back blows and chest thrusts
option_b: Begin infant CPR. Place on firm surface give 30 compressions look in mouth and remove visible object give 2 breaths. If breaths do not go in reposition and try again then continue CPR
option_c: Perform abdominal thrusts now that she is unconscious
option_d: Blind finger sweep to find object
rationale: Unconscious infant choking is treated with CPR. Place on firm surface and begin compressions. Look in mouth before each breath and remove visible objects only. Do not perform blind finger sweeps. Compressions may help dislodge the object.
